title: Bissara
subtitle: Moroccan dried fava bean soup
excerpt: Dried split fava beans cooked until fully soft, then blended to a thick, smooth soup and poured into bowls with a pool of olive oil, cumin, and paprika drizzled over the top. Street food of northern Morocco. Eaten for breakfast or as a warming winter lunch with bread.
sourceNotes: Bissara is the street food of the Rif and northern Morocco, sold from large pots at dawn from street carts for workers beginning the day. It is made from dried split fava beans (dried, peeled, and split broad beans) cooked until completely soft, then blended with olive oil, garlic, and cumin. Related to Egyptian bessara and Moroccan ful medames tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. Thin with water when reheating.

content.text: Split dried fava beans (peeled and halved) cook faster than whole dried favas and produce a smoother result. They do not need soaking, though soaking for an hour speeds up cooking. The beans should be cooked until completely collapsing — no texture should remain before blending.

The soup is thick, closer to a porridge than a broth. Thin it to your preferred consistency with more warm water. The olive oil drizzled on top at serving is not a garnish but a structural part of the dish — a full tablespoon per bowl, poured in a pool at the centre, with the cumin and paprika scattered on top.

items.prepNote: good quality, plus extra to serve

content.text: Put the {{dried-split-fava-beans}} and {{garlic}} in a large pot. Cover with 1.2 litres of cold water. Bring to a boil, skim any foam, then reduce to a simmer. Cook for 35 to 40 minutes until the beans are completely soft and beginning to fall apart. Add the {{cumin-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and half the {{olive-oil}}. Use a stick blender to blend until smooth. Add the {{lemon-juice}} and blend again. Adjust consistency with more warm water if needed — bissara should be thick but pourable. Taste and adjust seasoning. Ladle into bowls. Pour a generous tablespoon of {{olive-oil}} into the centre of each bowl. Sprinkle with {{paprika-sweet}} and more cumin. Finish with {{coriander}} if using. Serve with bread.

items.symptom: Beans not fully soft after 40 minutes
items.cause: Old dried beans or hard water
items.fix: Continue cooking in 10-minute increments. Very old dried beans can take up to 1 hour. Using filtered or soft water speeds up cooking
Blend in short pulses and stop when smooth. Over-processing releases excess starch and makes the texture gluey
Dried fava beans need more salt than expected. Taste after blending and again after resting — the flavour develops as the soup sits. The olive oil at serving amplifies all the flavours

content.text: Bissara is street food and working breakfast in northern Morocco, particularly in Tangier and Tetouan, where vendors set up before dawn. The large pot, the ladle, the drizzle of olive oil and dusting of cumin and paprika — the ritual of serving is as consistent as the recipe. It is cheap, nourishing, and eaten standing up at the side of the street with bread. At home it is a winter soup, made in a big batch and reheated through the week. The related Egyptian dish bessara uses a similar base but adds fresh herbs and has a different colour and texture.
